title: Asuka in Episode 22 Different Analyses
wikitext:
[]: Asuka in Episode 22: Different Analyses             Quote Post by Director Black » Sat Aug 20, 2016 4:10 pm I want to make this thread dedicated to a study and summarization of Asuka and her entire profile featured in the episode. One thing I noticed is that for the most part, people jump towards Asuka after they finish Episode 22. Personally, this episode made a large impact on me as I saw so much of myself in it during a deep depression. Trying to act strong after the death of a parent, wanting to grow up right away, standing out, the list goes on. But ignoring my rambling for the moment, I want to ask, what are your overarching thoughts and interpretations of the entire episode? Did it affect you in anyway or did you see yourself in Asuka like I did?

[]: Re: Asuka in Episode 22: Different Analyses             Quote Post by Guy Nacks » Sat Aug 20, 2016 8:09 pm I never connected with Asuka on a personal level at all; I am not, nor ever have been, a person with narcissistic tendencies. However, I can be sympathetic to her plight. What the episode does--in the same way that 23 does with Rei, to an extent--is reveal to the audience the true nature of the character, stripping away all surface tendencies and innuendo. Such a maneuver was to be expected as the series was nearing completion and certain characters had to have their arcs developed and sufficiently expanded upon and revealed before the series ended.

[]: Re: Asuka in Episode 22: Different Analyses             Quote Post by Director Black » Sat Aug 20, 2016 9:46 pm Guy Nacks wrote: I never connected with Asuka on a personal level at all; I am not, nor ever have been, a person with narcissistic tendencies. However, I can be sympathetic to her plight.

[]: Re: Asuka in Episode 22: Different Analyses             Quote Post by Reichu » Sun Aug 21, 2016 12:53 am I doubt those diagnosed "narcissistic" IRL are able to have their mind stripped down to a vulnerable, child-like state in order to decide whether or not they're ACTUALLY, REALLY narcissists. All that matters is the conclusion a trained professional observing the subject would make. And in any case, the defense mechanisms a person uses are very much part of who they "really are", in that they emerge organically from the underlying psychic strata. Also, being able to point to what made you sick doesn't make you any less sick.

[]: Re: Asuka in Episode 22: Different Analyses             Quote Post by Director Black » Sun Aug 21, 2016 1:26 am @Reichu You said better then I ever could. My wording on my last post was a bit shitty I'll admit. I will say this: if 3I never happened, Asuka would no doubt be a normal teenage girl. It's what makes me shiver when I hear her scream no, cause you know somewhere, there's a wonderful and outgoing (In the REAL sense and not in the fake sense) girl hiding herself from her low self-esteem. What's also brilliant about the whole scenario is that everyone is watching her get her mind defiled, showing the fragile girl she's trying to hide.

[]: Re: Asuka in Episode 22: Different Analyses             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Sun Aug 21, 2016 4:27 pm Director Black wrote: One thing I noticed is that for the most part, people jump towards Asuka after they finish Episode 22.

[]: Re: Asuka in Episode 22: Different Analyses             Quote Post by Director Black » Sun Aug 21, 2016 5:00 pm Mr. Tines wrote: The many intervening years have blurred my initial reactions, except that I know that I'm an outlier here, since it was episode 16 where I jumped, where first I saw some of my late-teen self in her. I quite honestly cannot remember my particular reactions to episode 22 rather than as part of the continued trend of kicking her when she was down. 2I, I presume you mean. And as normal as a girl who picked up her first degree at age 13 could be -- which is to say, still somewhat screwed up by her own success. Being a prodigy isn't a bed of roses.

[]: Re: Asuka in Episode 22: Different Analyses             Quote Post by NemZ » Sun Aug 21, 2016 6:13 pm Ep 22 moved me from "dislike" to "dislike and pity"... which would of course just piss Asuka off even more.

[]: Re: Asuka in Episode 22: Different Analyses             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Sun Aug 21, 2016 7:16 pm At first it seemed to be that Asuka was everything the show needed at the point in which she was introduced. While introspectiveness with Shinji and Rei was thoroughly interesting, the show was lacking the energy that was usually associated with shows of its kind. I knew Anno was going to do something interesting with the red-head that kept showing up in the show's OP, given his story with other red-head characters at the time like Jung Freud from Gunbuster, as well as Grandis and Marie from The Secret of Blue Water. And Asuka was all of the energy that a show like Evangelion is used to having for quite some time during the show. I actually grew fond of the character, despite the facade that she was obviously putting on. (Episode 11 did well enough to make it more apparent as to what kind of Character Asuka really was, both as a snob and as someone who genuinely cared about the rest of the pilots.) Episode 22 always got to me, though. It was one of my favorite episodes of the show simply due to its technical construction and how it used film to tell a story. But the episode's weight grew even stronger and more overwhelming after I marathoner the whole show in one sitting. It's a very violent episode. It's probably the most violent episode in Japanese animation that doesn't resort to conventional portrayals of violence, such as blood, bruises, or other forms of physical violence. I felt an almost physical pain after watching Episode 22 in the context of the marathon (the "Director's Cut," mind you), and hadn't been able to approach the episode the same way ever again. The episode explores how and why Asuka does things only for the benefit of herself, and recontextualizes the help that she offered her fellow pilots back in Episode 11, simply by unveiling her motivations for doing so. Much of that recontextualization, however, seemed to be the doing of the angel probing her mind, and not because of anything she honestly felt about herself. This is a psychological attack on Asuka, and thusly the Angel is bending and changing the context of her past good deeds in order to make whatever selfish desires she may have had in the past more prominent, while completely elimination any genuine concern she might have felt towards anyone at any given time. (We know these genuine emotions exist within Asuka because of how Episodes 25 and 26 device a more honest internalized discussion Asuka has with herself, dealing directly with why she does what she does and ultimately how she relates to others.)

[]: Re: Asuka in Episode 22: Different Analyses             Quote Post by Director Black » Sun Aug 21, 2016 8:56 pm I also would love to bring up the scene where Arael asks Asuka if she's lonely. The setting is a playground, and with multiple viewings, I gain a better understanding on why it's there. Asuka never had a chance to have a normal childhood, with both the EVA training and her being in college.

[]: Re: Asuka in Episode 22: Different Analyses             Quote Post by Reichu » Mon Aug 22, 2016 1:27 am It's interesting to contrast the playground scene with Rei's chat with Armisael. Both end with the Angel exposing the truth of the pilot's loneliness and leering about it, then the psychic contact abruptly ending. Despite Rei suffering from both physical AND mental violation, while Asuka only gets the latter, Rei's experience doesn't seem to be anywhere near as terrible and she actually acknowledges the Angel as an equal (before blowing it up because she cares about Shinji more). I wonder if Asuka's experience with Arael would have been much less painful if she weren't in denial about so very, very much. FreakyFilmFan4ever wrote: This is a physiological attack on Asuka

[]: Re: Asuka in Episode 22: Different Analyses             Quote Post by Director Black » Mon Aug 22, 2016 8:08 am Reichu wrote: I wonder if Asuka's experience with Arael would have been much less painful if she weren't in denial about so very, very much.

[]: Re: Asuka in Episode 22: Different Analyses             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Mon Aug 22, 2016 7:24 pm Reichu wrote: Sorry, I have to be "that guy". The attack is on Asuka's psyche (mind and soul), hence it is psycho logical. "Physiological" looks sort of similar, but it refers to the (comparatively more concretely understood) physical workings of the body. Metabolism is an example of a physiological process.

[]: Re: Asuka in Episode 22: Different Analyses             Quote Post by CommanderFish » Wed Aug 24, 2016 4:33 pm I've wanted to post on this thread for a while now because I love this episode, but I just don't know what I could say that hasn't already been said. I guess... I'll talk about the visuals. To me, eva's storytelling is often highly emotional; by which I mean, it's really good at conveying a feeling to the viewer. It's also very cerebral, yes; but really catching on to a lot of the more complex, psychological, and philosophical elements require either multiple viewings or--more often--reflection after an episode. A feeling, however, can be delivered immediately--in its complete form (i.e. with no extra thought required) straight into the viewer's head. That's important, because feelings and emotions can serve as a gateway into understanding deeper thoughts and ideas, (which lead to more emotions, which lead to more ideas, etc.). Anyways, one way that I believe eva portrays emotions/feelings very well is through its visuals. Often-times the visuals on screen do a great job of delivering a feeling of uneasiness and confusion: they're weird, unsettling, and--at least upon a first-time viewing--the viewer doesn't really know what's exactly going on. Asuka's mind-rape in no exception to this. Unit-02 writhing in pain under Arael's light-beam, the doll hanging by a noose in a bright-red room, Asuka trying to hide herself under a spotlight, the red-and-black sea of ambiguous figures smothering her: all of these things establish a certain level of uncomfortability, perhaps even fear within the viewer, and I think eva does this especially well (even if I can't really explain why; the visuals are just extremely powerful to me, I guess). This emotional establishment is vital, because it allows the audio-section of the storytelling to come in and elaborate on all these feelings: adding more emotion on top of that, providing a greater understanding, contrasting ideas, etc. During Asuka's mind-rape, her lines allow you to get a specific idea of her past, how she became the person she is now, how she hides her true self, and how this all culminates in stripping down her character to reveal her greatest fear: being dependent on someone (what a beautiful scene). But to really understand her character, you need to at least understand some of what she is feeling; you need to understand her fear. Everything shown during that scene, everything that made you feel uncomfortable or unsettled; that's what allows the viewer to start to attain a greater understanding of what Asuka is feeling ; and--for me, personally--this was done quite successfully. I could talk about this scene more, such as how it serves the overall narrative of the series, but I don't know how much sense I made with this post, so I don't want to go too much further. I may have over-explained some things that most would already understand, but... whatever. I'm getting writing practice.

[]: Re: Asuka in Episode 22: Different Analyses             Quote Post by Tumbling Down » Wed Aug 24, 2016 6:41 pm FreakyFilmFan4ever wrote: The episode explores how and why Asuka does things only for the benefit of herself, and recontextualizes the help that she offered her fellow pilots back in Episode 11, simply by unveiling her motivations for doing so. Much of that recontextualization, however, seemed to be the doing of the angel probing her mind, and not because of anything she honestly felt about herself.

[]: Re: Asuka in Episode 22: Different Analyses             Quote Post by Sachi » Wed Aug 24, 2016 7:31 pm Unfortunately for me, I never saw the DC ep 22 until quite some time after my intial viewing (which is strange, because I owned each of the other DC episodes on DVD), and so I missed out on a lot of the stuff regarding Asuka's feelings for Shinji. However, I had always liked Asuka from the start, so seeing her breakdown made me rather sympathetic, however what really got me was her absolute inability to do anything in the next episode as a result, and then her eventual suicide attempt. Her downhill spiral began with Bardiel and was emphasized with Zeruel; Arael was the next logical step, and Armisael was the nail in the coffin. I think back then, I was upset at the lack of Unit-01 in the episode, which also took away from my experience initially. I was 12 or so and focused a lot on the action at the time; after Zeruel, I just wanted more and more Unit-01 (and received the exact opposite of that).

[]: Re: Asuka in Episode 22: Different Analyses             Quote Post by CommanderFish » Wed Aug 24, 2016 9:46 pm Tumbling Down wrote: You were explaining yourself fine, dude! Continue! Unless you don't feel comfortable, that is.

[]: Re: Asuka in Episode 22: Different Analyses             Quote Post by Director Black » Thu Aug 25, 2016 9:44 pm CommanderFish wrote: Often-times the visuals on screen do a great job of delivering a feeling of uneasiness and confusion: they're weird, unsettling, and--at least upon a first-time viewing--the viewer doesn't really know what's exactly going on. Asuka's mind-rape in no exception to this.

[]: Re: Asuka in Episode 22: Different Analyses             Quote Post by Director Black » Thu Aug 25, 2016 10:21 pm Tumbling Down wrote: While Asuka obviously wasn't the confident person she pretended to be, I thought she truly was as self-centered as she came across.

[]: Re: Asuka in Episode 22: Different Analyses             Quote Post by CommanderFish » Fri Aug 26, 2016 12:18 am Director Black wrote: Probably because many people don't learn through visuals. That's probably one of the main reasons I love Eva so much. I was able to digest many of the metaphors and images relating to Asuka's struggle.
